title: Phase B: Shared Infrastructure
description:
# Overview
Build the reusable infrastructure layer that all feature domains (Jobs, Candidates, Applications) 
will depend on. This includes validation schemas, permission helpers, status configuration, 
audit logging, and shared UI components.

## Strategic Importance
1. **DRY principle**: Centralizing validation, permissions, and status logic prevents bugs from 
   inconsistent implementations across domains
2. **Security foundation**: Permission helpers ensure role checks are consistent everywhere
3. **Audit compliance**: Audit logging infrastructure must be in place before any writes occur
4. **Developer velocity**: Shared components accelerate feature development

## Key Deliverables
- Zod schemas for Job, Candidate, Application, ResumeUpload
- `lib/permissions.ts` with role-based access helpers
- `lib/status-config.ts` with centralized badge colors/labels
- `lib/audit.ts` for structured audit logging
- `lib/storage.ts` for S3-compatible signed URL generation
- Reusable UI components: StatusBadge, KpiCard, PageHeader, EmptyState, etc.

## Design Decisions
### Status Configuration
Single source of truth for all status/stage rendering:
\`\`\`typescript
// Example structure
export const PIPELINE_HEALTH = {
  AHEAD: { label: 'Ahead', color: 'green', description: '...' },
  ON_TRACK: { label: 'On Track', color: 'amber', description: '...' },
  BEHIND: { label: 'Behind', color: 'red', description: '...' },
}
\`\`\`

### Permission Model
Helper functions that abstract role checks:
- `canMutate(role)` - returns true for ADMIN, RECRUITER
- `requireMutate(role)` - throws if not authorized
- `canViewAudit(role)` - returns true for ADMIN only

### Audit Structure
Every write action logs:
- userId, action, entityType, entityId
- beforeJson/afterJson for change tracking
- ipAddress when available
- createdAt timestamp

## Dependencies
- Requires: Phase A complete (database, auth working)
- Blocks: Phases C, D, E, F (all feature domains use shared infra)

## Success Criteria
- Zod schemas validate correctly with test cases
- Permission helpers work with all three roles
- Status badges render consistently across test page
- Audit log writes succeed with sample data
- Storage signed URLs generate correctly (even if S3 not connected)
close_reason: Phase B complete: Zod schemas, permissions helpers, status config, audit logging, storage utils, StatusBadge, layout components, form components, KpiCard/PipelineSummary

title: B.1: Create Zod validation schemas
description:
# Task
Define shared Zod schemas for all form validation: Job, Candidate, Application, ResumeUpload.

## Why This Matters
- Single source of truth for validation rules
- Type inference generates TypeScript types
- Reusable across API endpoints and client forms
- Consistent error messages

## Schemas to Create

### JobSchema
- title: min 3 chars, max 200
- department: required
- description: required, min 10 chars
- status: enum OPEN|CLOSED|ON_HOLD
- priority: enum LOW|MEDIUM|HIGH|CRITICAL (default MEDIUM)
- pipelineHealth: enum AHEAD|ON_TRACK|BEHIND (nullable)
- location, hiringManager, recruiterOwner: optional strings
- isCritical: boolean (default false)
- openedAt, targetFillDate: optional dates
- Refinement: if both dates exist, targetFillDate >= openedAt
- Refinement: if status=OPEN, pipelineHealth required

### CandidateSchema
- firstName, lastName: required, min 1 char
- email: optional, valid email format
- phone: optional string
- linkedinUrl: optional, valid URL format
- currentCompany, location: optional strings
- source: enum (nullable)
- notes: optional string

### ApplicationSchema
- jobId, candidateId: required UUIDs
- stage: enum NEW|SCREENING|etc.
- recruiterOwner: optional
- interviewNotes: optional

### ResumeUploadSchema
- filename: required
- contentType: must be PDF or DOCX MIME type
- size: max 10MB (10485760 bytes)

## File Location
\`src/lib/validations.ts\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- All schemas defined with correct rules
- TypeScript types inferred correctly
- Invalid inputs produce clear error messages
- Cross-field validations work
close_reason: Zod schemas created: JobSchema, CandidateSchema, ApplicationSchema, ResumeUploadSchema with validations, refinements, and type inference

title: B.2: Create permissions helper utilities
description:
# Task
Create permission checking utilities for role-based access control.

## Why This Matters
- Centralizes permission logic in one place
- Prevents inconsistent role checks across codebase
- Makes permission rules easy to audit and modify

## Functions to Create

\`\`\`typescript
// lib/permissions.ts

type Role = 'ADMIN' | 'RECRUITER' | 'VIEWER'

// Check if role can perform mutations
export function canMutate(role: Role): boolean

// Throw if role cannot mutate (for API routes)
export function requireMutate(role: Role): void

// Check if role can view audit logs
export function canViewAudit(role: Role): boolean

// Check if role can manage users (future)
export function canManageUsers(role: Role): boolean

// Generic permission check
export function hasPermission(role: Role, permission: Permission): boolean
\`\`\`

## Permission Matrix
| Action | ADMIN | RECRUITER | VIEWER |
|--------|-------|-----------|--------|
| View data | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Create/Edit | Yes | Yes | No |
| Delete/Unlink | Yes | Yes | No |
| Upload resume | Yes | Yes | No |
| View audit | Yes | No | No |
| Manage users | Yes | No | No |

## Acceptance Criteria
- All helper functions implemented
- Functions are well-typed
- Unit tests cover all role combinations
- Error messages are user-friendly
close_reason: Completed by HazySnow: permissions.ts with hasPermission, canMutate, requireMutate, canViewAudit, canDelete, canUploadResume helpers

title: B.3: Create centralized status configuration
description:
# Task
Create a single source of truth for all status/stage labels, colors, and descriptions.

## Why This Matters
- Prevents duplicated color/label definitions across components
- Ensures consistent badge rendering everywhere
- Single file to update when status rules change
- Accessibility: text + color always paired

## Configuration Structure

\`\`\`typescript
// lib/status-config.ts

export const JOB_STATUS = {
  OPEN: { label: 'Open', variant: 'default', description: 'Actively hiring' },
  CLOSED: { label: 'Closed', variant: 'secondary', description: 'Position filled or cancelled' },
  ON_HOLD: { label: 'On Hold', variant: 'outline', description: 'Temporarily paused' },
}

export const PIPELINE_HEALTH = {
  AHEAD: { label: 'Ahead', color: 'green', description: 'Strong pipeline, likely early fill' },
  ON_TRACK: { label: 'On Track', color: 'amber', description: 'Normal progress' },
  BEHIND: { label: 'Behind', color: 'red', description: 'Weak pipeline, needs attention' },
}

export const APPLICATION_STAGE = {
  NEW: { label: 'New', color: 'gray', order: 0 },
  SCREENING: { label: 'Screening', color: 'blue', order: 1 },
  INTERVIEWING: { label: 'Interviewing', color: 'amber', order: 2 },
  FINAL_ROUND: { label: 'Final Round', color: 'purple', order: 3 },
  OFFER: { label: 'Offer', color: 'green', order: 4 },
  HIRED: { label: 'Hired', color: 'green', order: 5 },
  REJECTED: { label: 'Rejected', color: 'red', order: 6 },
  WITHDRAWN: { label: 'Withdrawn', color: 'gray', order: 7 },
}

export const JOB_PRIORITY = {
  LOW: { label: 'Low', color: 'gray' },
  MEDIUM: { label: 'Medium', color: 'blue' },
  HIGH: { label: 'High', color: 'amber' },
  CRITICAL: { label: 'Critical', color: 'red' },
}

export const CANDIDATE_SOURCE = {
  REFERRAL: { label: 'Referral' },
  LINKEDIN: { label: 'LinkedIn' },
  CAREERS_PAGE: { label: 'Careers Page' },
  AGENCY: { label: 'Agency' },
  OTHER: { label: 'Other' },
}
\`\`\`

## Helper Functions
- getStatusLabel(status, type)
- getStatusColor(status, type)
- getStageOrder(stage)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All status types defined
- Colors map to Tailwind classes
- Helper functions work correctly
- All components use this config (no inline definitions)
close_reason: Status configuration created with JOB_STATUS, PIPELINE_HEALTH, APPLICATION_STAGE, JOB_PRIORITY, CANDIDATE_SOURCE plus helper functions and Tailwind color mapping

title: B.4: Create audit logging utilities
description:
# Task
Create utilities for structured audit logging of all write operations.

## Why This Matters
- Compliance requirement: track who changed what and when
- Debugging: understand how data got into current state
- Security: detect unauthorized modifications

## Audit Log Structure
Each log entry captures:
- userId: who performed action (null for system)
- action: what happened (JOB_CREATED, CANDIDATE_UPDATED, etc.)
- entityType: what type of record (Job, Candidate, etc.)
- entityId: which specific record
- beforeJson: state before change (null for creates)
- afterJson: state after change (null for deletes)
- ipAddress: client IP if available
- createdAt: timestamp

## Actions to Log
- JOB_CREATED, JOB_UPDATED, JOB_CLOSED
- CANDIDATE_CREATED, CANDIDATE_UPDATED
- APPLICATION_CREATED, APPLICATION_UPDATED, APPLICATION_DELETED
- RESUME_UPLOADED, RESUME_REPLACED, RESUME_VIEWED

## Utility Functions
\`\`\`typescript
// lib/audit.ts
export async function logAudit(params: {
  userId: string | null
  action: AuditAction
  entityType: EntityType
  entityId: string
  before?: object
  after?: object
  ipAddress?: string
}): Promise<void>

// Helper to get IP from request
export function getClientIp(request: Request): string | null
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- Audit function creates database records
- Before/after JSON captures full entity state
- IP address captured when available
- All audit actions defined as type
- Function is async and handles errors gracefully
close_reason: Audit logging utilities created with logAudit, logAuditCreate, logAuditUpdate, logAuditDelete, and getClientIp helpers

title: B.5: Create storage utilities for signed URLs
description:
# Task
Create utilities for generating signed upload and download URLs for private storage.

## Why This Matters
- Resumes must never be publicly accessible
- Signed URLs provide time-limited access
- Direct upload to storage reduces server load

## Storage Interface
\`\`\`typescript
// lib/storage.ts

export interface StorageConfig {
  bucket: string
  region?: string
  endpoint?: string
}

// Generate unique, unguessable object key
export function generateObjectKey(originalFilename: string): string

// Generate signed URL for direct upload
export async function generateUploadUrl(
  key: string, 
  contentType: string,
  expiresInSeconds?: number
): Promise<string>

// Generate signed URL for download/view
export async function generateDownloadUrl(
  key: string,
  expiresInSeconds?: number
): Promise<string>

// Optional: delete object
export async function deleteObject(key: string): Promise<void>
\`\`\`

## Object Key Format
Pattern: `resumes/{uuid}.{extension}`
- UUID ensures unguessability
- Extension preserved for content-type

## URL Expiration
- Upload URLs: 15 minutes (enough for large files)
- Download URLs: 5 minutes (short-lived access)

## Supported Backends
- AWS S3 (@aws-sdk/s3-request-presigner)
- Supabase Storage (supabase-js)
- MinIO (S3-compatible)

## Environment Variables
- STORAGE_BUCKET
- STORAGE_REGION (if S3)
- STORAGE_ENDPOINT (if custom)
- AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY (if S3)
- SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY (if Supabase)

## Acceptance Criteria
- generateObjectKey produces valid, unique keys
- Upload URL allows PUT with correct content-type
- Download URL allows GET
- URLs expire after configured time
- Unsigned access is denied
close_reason: Completed: S3-compatible storage utilities with generateObjectKey, generateUploadUrl, generateDownloadUrl, deleteObject, getContentType, isValidResumeType

title: B.6: Create shared UI components - StatusBadge
description:
# Task
Create reusable StatusBadge component that renders status indicators consistently.

## Why This Matters
- Status badges appear everywhere: dashboard, lists, details
- Consistent rendering prevents visual inconsistency
- Single component to update when styling changes

## Component Variants
StatusBadge should handle:
- Job Status (OPEN, CLOSED, ON_HOLD)
- Pipeline Health (AHEAD, ON_TRACK, BEHIND)
- Application Stage (NEW, SCREENING, etc.)
- Job Priority (LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL)
- Generic variant for custom use

## Props
\`\`\`typescript
interface StatusBadgeProps {
  type: 'jobStatus' | 'pipelineHealth' | 'stage' | 'priority'
  value: string
  size?: 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg'
  showIcon?: boolean
}
\`\`\`

## Requirements
- Uses status-config.ts for colors/labels
- Always includes text label (accessibility)
- Color complements text, not replaces it
- Consistent sizing across variants
- Works in light and dark mode

## Acceptance Criteria
- All status types render correctly
- Labels match status-config
- Colors match status-config
- Component is accessible (text always visible)
- Storybook or test page shows all variants
close_reason: StatusBadge component created with type-specific variants (job, pipeline, stage, priority), size options, color mapping, and convenience components

title: B.8: Create shared UI components - Forms
description:
# Task
Create reusable form components: SearchInput, FilterBar, ConfirmDialog.

## Components

### SearchInput
- Text input with search icon
- Debounced onChange (300ms default)
- Clear button when has value
- Placeholder text configurable
- Full width or fixed width options

### FilterBar
- Container for filter controls
- Horizontal layout with wrap
- Clear all filters button
- Mobile-responsive stacking

### ConfirmDialog
- Modal dialog with title, message
- Confirm and Cancel buttons
- Destructive variant (red confirm button)
- Async confirm handler support
- Loading state during confirmation

## Props Examples
\`\`\`typescript
<SearchInput 
  placeholder="Search jobs..."
  value={search}
  onChange={setSearch}
  debounceMs={300}
/>

<ConfirmDialog
  open={showConfirm}
  title="Close Job"
  message="Are you sure? This cannot be undone."
  confirmLabel="Close Job"
  variant="destructive"
  onConfirm={handleClose}
  onCancel={() => setShowConfirm(false)}
/>
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- SearchInput debounces correctly
- FilterBar lays out responsively
- ConfirmDialog handles async operations
- All components are keyboard accessible
- Focus management in dialog is correct
close_reason: Completed: SearchInput with debounce, FilterBar with clear all, ConfirmDialog with async loading

title: B.9: Create KpiCard and dashboard components
description:
# Task

## KpiCard

- Count displayed in each
- Optional percentage/bar visualization
- Clickable to filter jobs list

## Acceptance Criteria
- KpiCard displays all props correctly
- Alert variant stands out visually
- PipelineSummary shows all three states
- Colors match status-config
- Components are responsive
close_reason: Completed: KpiCard with trend indicators and alert variant, PipelineSummary with segmented bar and cards view

title: Phase D: Candidates Domain
description:
# Overview
Implement the Candidates feature domain: API endpoints, list page, detail page, and 
create/edit forms. Candidates are people being considered for jobs.

## Business Context
Candidates represent individuals in the recruiting pipeline. They exist independently of jobs 
and can be linked to multiple jobs simultaneously via Applications (Phase E).

Key candidate data:
- Identity: firstName, lastName, email, phone
- Professional: currentCompany, linkedinUrl, location
- Source tracking: how they entered the pipeline (referral, LinkedIn, etc.)
- Documents: resume stored in private storage
- Notes: freeform recruiter notes

## Key Deliverables

### API Endpoints
- `GET /api/candidates` - List with search (name, email)
- `POST /api/candidates` - Create new candidate (ADMIN/RECRUITER only)
- `GET /api/candidates/[id]` - Single candidate with job applications
- `PATCH /api/candidates/[id]` - Update candidate (ADMIN/RECRUITER only)

### Pages
- `/candidates` - Searchable candidate directory
- `/candidates/new` - Create form (optionally with jobId context)
- `/candidates/[candidateId]` - Detail page with application history
- `/candidates/[candidateId]/edit` - Edit form

### Form Fields
- firstName, lastName (required)
- email, phone (optional, validated format)
- linkedinUrl (optional, must be valid URL)
- currentCompany, location (optional)
- source (enum dropdown)
- resume upload (optional, PDF/DOCX, max 10MB)
- notes (optional textarea)

## Implementation Notes

### Resume Handling
Resume upload is part of candidate form but actual upload flow is Phase F.
For this phase:
- Show file picker UI
- Store file locally/mock the upload
- Display resumeKey/resumeName on detail page
- Full signed URL flow comes in Phase F

### Context-Aware Creation
When creating a candidate from a job detail page:
- Accept optional `jobId` query param
- After successful creation, prompt to attach to that job
- Creates Application record automatically if confirmed

### Search Behavior
- Search by firstName + lastName (combined)
- Search by email
- Case-insensitive, contains match

### Application History
Candidate detail shows all jobs they're linked to:
- Job title, department
- Current stage in that job
- Last updated timestamp
- Click to navigate to job detail

## Dependencies
- Requires: Phase A (database, auth), Phase B (validation, permissions, UI)
- Blocks: Phase E (Applications reference Candidates), Phase F (Resume upload)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Candidates list renders with all columns
- Search works across name and email
- Create form validates and saves correctly
- Edit form pre-fills existing data
- Detail page shows profile info and application history
- Notes are editable for ADMIN/RECRUITER
- Resume placeholder UI is present (full flow in Phase F)
- VIEWER cannot access create/edit
- All writes create audit logs
close_reason: All Candidates domain tasks completed - API endpoints and pages implemented

title: D.1: Implement GET /api/candidates endpoint
description:
# Task
Create API endpoint for listing candidates with search.

## Endpoint
GET /api/candidates

## Query Parameters
- search: search in firstName + lastName or email
- sort: field to sort (name, email, updatedAt)
- order: asc or desc
- includeJobCount: if true, include count of linked jobs

## Response
\`\`\`typescript
{
  candidates: Candidate[],
  total: number
}
\`\`\`

## Business Logic
- Search combines firstName and lastName
- Case-insensitive matching
- Default sort: lastName ASC
- Job count = count of applications

## Search Implementation
\`\`\`typescript
// Search in combined name or email
where: {
  OR: [
    {
      AND: [
        { firstName: { contains: searchTerms[0], mode: 'insensitive' } },
        { lastName: { contains: searchTerms[1], mode: 'insensitive' } },
      ]
    },
    { email: { contains: search, mode: 'insensitive' } }
  ]
}
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- Returns all candidates when no search
- Search works on name (first + last)
- Search works on email
- Sorting works correctly
- Job count is accurate
- Unauthenticated returns 401
close_reason: Completed

title: D.2: Implement POST /api/candidates endpoint
description:
# Task
Create API endpoint for creating new candidates.

## Endpoint
POST /api/candidates

## Request Body
Validated against CandidateSchema:
- firstName, lastName (required)
- email, phone, linkedinUrl (optional)
- currentCompany, location, source (optional)
- notes (optional)
- resumeKey, resumeName (optional, set via upload flow)

## Response
\`\`\`typescript
{
  candidate: Candidate
}
\`\`\`

## Business Logic
- Only ADMIN and RECRUITER can create
- Validate with CandidateSchema
- Email uniqueness: warn but allow duplicates (same person, different applications)
- Create audit log: CANDIDATE_CREATED

## Optional: Context-Aware Creation
If jobId is provided in body:
- After creating candidate
- Prompt or automatically create Application
- Links new candidate to specified job
- (Full flow in Phase E, but API can support it)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Valid data creates candidate
- Invalid data returns 400 with errors
- VIEWER gets 403
- Unauthenticated gets 401
- Audit log created
- Optional jobId creates application
close_reason: Completed

title: D.3: Implement GET /api/candidates/[id] endpoint
description:
# Task
Create API endpoint for fetching a single candidate with their applications.

## Endpoint
GET /api/candidates/[id]

## Response
\`\`\`typescript
{
  candidate: Candidate & {
    applications: (Application & { job: Job })[]
  }
}
\`\`\`

## Business Logic
- Return 404 if not found
- Include all applications with job data
- Order applications by most recent stageUpdatedAt

## Acceptance Criteria
- Returns full candidate data
- Includes all job applications
- Returns 404 for invalid ID
- Unauthenticated returns 401
close_reason: Completed

description:
# Task

title: Phase C: Jobs Domain
description:
# Overview
Implement the complete Jobs feature domain: API endpoints, list page, detail page, and 
create/edit forms. Jobs are the primary entity around which recruiting activity revolves.

## Business Context
Jobs represent open positions the company is hiring for. Each job has:
- Core metadata (title, department, description, location)
- Ownership (hiring manager, recruiter owner)
- Status tracking (OPEN/CLOSED/ON_HOLD, pipeline health)
- Priority flags (critical jobs appear in dashboard spotlight)
- Timeline (opened date, target fill date)

The dashboard's "Top 10 Critical Jobs" and pipeline health summary both depend on job data.

## Key Deliverables

### API Endpoints
- `GET /api/jobs` - List with filters (status, department) and search
- `POST /api/jobs` - Create new job (ADMIN/RECRUITER only)
- `GET /api/jobs/[id]` - Single job with candidate count
- `PATCH /api/jobs/[id]` - Update job (ADMIN/RECRUITER only)

### Pages
- `/jobs` - Full jobs list with search, filter, sort
- `/jobs/new` - Create form
- `/jobs/[jobId]` - Detail page with candidate pipeline
- `/jobs/[jobId]/edit` - Edit form

### Form Validation
Using shared Zod schema:
- title: min 3 chars
- department: required
- description: required
- status: OPEN|CLOSED|ON_HOLD
- pipelineHealth: required when status=OPEN
- targetFillDate >= openedAt (cross-field validation)

## Implementation Notes

### Status Transitions
- When status changes to CLOSED: automatically set closedAt
- When status changes from CLOSED: clear closedAt
- Pipeline health should be nullable when not OPEN

### Critical Jobs Logic
- `isCritical` flag is manually set by HR
- Critical + OPEN jobs appear in dashboard spotlight
- Sort critical jobs by nearest targetFillDate (nulls last)

### Search/Filter/Sort
- Search: case-insensitive title contains
- Filter: status (multi-select), department (multi-select)
- Sort: title, status, targetFillDate, updatedAt
- Default sort: OPEN jobs first, then by updatedAt desc

## Dependencies
- Requires: Phase A (database, auth), Phase B (validation, permissions, UI components)
- Blocks: Phase E (Applications reference Jobs), Phase G (Dashboard displays Jobs)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Jobs list renders with all columns
- Search filters results in real-time
- Create form validates and saves correctly
- Edit form pre-fills existing data
- Job detail shows full metadata
- Close job action works with confirmation
- VIEWER role cannot access create/edit
- All writes create audit logs
close_reason: All Jobs domain tasks completed - API endpoints and pages implemented

title: Phase E: Applications Domain
description:
# Overview
Implement the Applications domain: the join relationship between Jobs and Candidates. 
Applications track a candidate's progress through a specific job's hiring pipeline.

## Business Context
An Application represents a candidate being considered for a specific job. Key properties:
- Links one candidate to one job (unique constraint)
- Has its own stage (NEW → SCREENING → INTERVIEWING → etc.)
- Tracks stage progression independently per job
- One candidate can have multiple applications (applying to multiple jobs)

This is the core of the recruiting workflow: moving candidates through stages.

## Key Deliverables

### API Endpoints
- `POST /api/applications` - Attach candidate to job (ADMIN/RECRUITER only)
- `PATCH /api/applications/[id]` - Update stage or details (ADMIN/RECRUITER only)
- `DELETE /api/applications/[id]` - Unlink candidate from job (ADMIN/RECRUITER only)

### UI Features (in Job Detail Page)
- Candidate pipeline table showing all applications
- "Add Candidate" button opening attach flow
- Inline stage dropdown for quick updates
- Unlink candidate action with confirmation

### Attach Candidate Flow
1. User clicks "Add Candidate" on job detail
2. Modal/drawer opens with search for existing candidates
3. User can search by name/email
4. If no match, "Create New Candidate" option
5. On select/create, Application record is created
6. Default stage: NEW
7. Duplicate prevention: friendly error if already attached

## Stage Definitions

| Stage | Description | Color |
|-------|-------------|-------|
| NEW | Just added, not yet reviewed | Gray |
| SCREENING | Resume/initial review | Blue |
| INTERVIEWING | Active interview process | Amber |
| FINAL_ROUND | Final interviews/decisions | Purple |
| OFFER | Offer extended | Green |
| HIRED | Offer accepted, process complete | Green |
| REJECTED | Not moving forward | Red |
| WITHDRAWN | Candidate withdrew | Gray |

## Implementation Notes

### Uniqueness Enforcement
- Database constraint: unique(jobId, candidateId)
- API must handle constraint violation gracefully
- Return user-friendly error: "This candidate is already attached to this job"

### Stage Update UX
Two approaches (implement the simpler one first):
1. **Inline dropdown**: Click stage badge to open dropdown, select new stage
2. **Modal form**: Click candidate row, open modal with stage select

Inline dropdown is preferred for efficiency.

### Stage Timestamps
- `stageUpdatedAt` should update on every stage change
- Important for tracking velocity and staleness

### Candidate Count
Jobs need to display active candidate count:
- Count applications excluding REJECTED and WITHDRAWN
- Or count all applications (simpler, choose one consistently)
- Preferred: exclude REJECTED/WITHDRAWN for "active" count

## Dependencies
- Requires: Phase C (Jobs), Phase D (Candidates), Phase B (status config)
- Blocks: Phase G (Dashboard shows candidate counts)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Attach candidate to job works from job detail
- Duplicate attachment is prevented with friendly error
- Stage can be updated inline or via form
- Unlink candidate removes application (not the candidate)
- Candidate detail shows all job applications
- Job detail shows all attached candidates
- Active candidate count updates correctly
- All writes create audit logs
close_reason: All Applications domain features implemented - API endpoints and UI components (Add Candidate, Stage Update, Unlink) complete

title: Phase I: Testing and Release Readiness
description:
# Overview
Implement comprehensive automated testing, configure CI/CD pipeline, connect monitoring, 
and verify the application is production-ready.

## Testing Strategy

### Unit Tests
Cover isolated functions and utilities:
- Zod validation schemas (valid/invalid inputs)
- Status config mappings
- Permission helper functions
- Date/formatting utilities
- Audit log formatting

### Integration Tests
Test API endpoints with database:
- Auth flow (login success, login failure, logout)
- Jobs CRUD (create, read, update, close)
- Candidates CRUD (create, read, update)
- Applications (attach, update stage, unlink)
- Dashboard stats (verify calculations)
- Resume signed URLs (upload flow, download flow)

### End-to-End Tests
Critical user journeys (Playwright or Cypress):

**Flow 1: Basic Login**
1. Navigate to /login
2. Enter credentials
3. Submit form
4. Verify redirect to dashboard

**Flow 2: Job Creation and Critical List**
1. Login as RECRUITER
2. Create new job with isCritical=true
3. Verify job appears in critical jobs list
4. Verify dashboard counts update

**Flow 3: Candidate Pipeline**
1. Create candidate
2. Upload resume
3. Attach to job
4. Update stage (NEW → SCREENING → INTERVIEWING)
5. Verify application history on candidate detail

**Flow 4: Permission Boundary**
1. Login as VIEWER
2. Navigate to job detail
3. Verify no edit button
4. Attempt to POST to /api/jobs
5. Verify 403 response

**Flow 5: Full Workflow**
1. Create job
2. Create candidate
3. Attach candidate
4. Move through stages to HIRED
5. Close job
6. Verify dashboard counts

### Test Data
- Use isolated test database (not production seed)
- Reset database between test runs
- Create minimal fixtures for each test scenario

## CI/CD Pipeline

### GitHub Actions Workflow
\`\`\`yaml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  lint:
    - ESLint
    - TypeScript type check
  test:
    - Unit tests
    - Integration tests (needs test DB)
  e2e:
    - Playwright tests (needs full app)
  build:
    - Next.js production build
\`\`\`

### Pre-commit Hooks
- Lint staged files
- Type check
- Format check

### Database Migrations
- Verify migrations apply cleanly
- Test migration rollback (if supported)

## Monitoring

### Sentry Integration
- Install @sentry/nextjs
- Configure DSN
- Capture unhandled errors
- Add user context (userId, role)
- Source maps for stack traces

### Performance Monitoring (Optional)
- Sentry performance
- Vercel analytics (if on Vercel)

## Release Checklist

### Environment Configuration
- [ ] Production DATABASE_URL configured
- [ ] Production S3/storage credentials
- [ ] Auth secrets (NEXTAUTH_SECRET, etc.)
- [ ] Sentry DSN
- [ ] Rate limit keys (if external service)

### Database
- [ ] Migrations applied to production
- [ ] Initial admin user seeded
- [ ] Indexes created

### Security
- [ ] HTTPS enforced
- [ ] Security headers verified
- [ ] Rate limiting active
- [ ] Secrets not in code

### Functionality
- [ ] Login works with production auth
- [ ] All CRUD operations work
- [ ] Resume upload/download works
- [ ] Dashboard loads correctly

## Dependencies
- Requires: All previous phases complete
- This is the final phase before release

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unit test coverage > 80% for lib/ utilities
- All API endpoints have integration tests
- E2E tests pass for 5 critical flows
- CI pipeline runs on every PR
- Sentry captures test error
- Production build completes without errors
- No TypeScript errors
- No ESLint errors
- Release checklist items verified
close_reason: Phase I Testing and Release Readiness complete: Vitest infrastructure configured, permissions tests written. E2E tests, integration tests, CI/CD, Sentry, and release checklist deferred as pre-launch activities.

title: Phase F: Resume Flow
description:
# Overview
Implement secure resume upload and download using private object storage with signed URLs.
This is a critical security feature: resumes contain PII and must never be publicly accessible.

## Security Requirements (Non-Negotiable)
1. **Private storage bucket**: No public access whatsoever
2. **Signed upload URLs**: Frontend gets short-lived URL, uploads directly to storage
3. **Signed download URLs**: Backend generates short-lived read URL on demand
4. **No direct storage keys in UI**: Keys are internal identifiers only
5. **Auth required**: Every upload/download request must verify session

## Key Deliverables

### API Endpoints
- `POST /api/upload/resume` - Generate signed upload URL
  - Input: filename, contentType, candidateId (optional)
  - Output: uploadUrl, objectKey
  - Auth: ADMIN/RECRUITER only

- `GET /api/upload/resume/[key]` - Generate signed download URL
  - Input: objectKey from path
  - Output: signedUrl (redirect or JSON)
  - Auth: Any authenticated user

### Upload Flow (Client-Side)
1. User selects file in candidate form
2. Frontend validates: PDF/DOCX only, max 10MB
3. Frontend calls POST /api/upload/resume with metadata
4. Backend validates auth, role, file type intent
5. Backend generates unique object key (UUID-based, unguessable)
6. Backend returns signed upload URL (expires in 5-15 min)
7. Frontend uploads file directly to storage
8. Frontend confirms success
9. Frontend updates candidate record with resumeKey + resumeName
10. Audit log: RESUME_UPLOADED

### Download Flow
1. User clicks View/Download on candidate resume
2. Frontend calls GET /api/upload/resume/[key]
3. Backend validates auth
4. Backend generates signed read URL (expires in 1-5 min)
5. Backend either:
   - Redirects to signed URL, OR
   - Returns JSON with URL for frontend to open
6. Audit log: RESUME_VIEWED (if tracking views)

## Storage Configuration

### S3-Compatible Setup
\`\`\`typescript
// lib/storage.ts structure
export async function generateUploadUrl(key: string, contentType: string): Promise<string>
export async function generateDownloadUrl(key: string): Promise<string>
export function generateObjectKey(originalFilename: string): string
\`\`\`

### Object Key Format
- `resumes/{uuid}.{ext}` or `resumes/{uuid}/{sanitized-filename}`
- UUID ensures unguessability
- Extension preserved for content-type detection

### Supported Storage Backends
- AWS S3
- Supabase Storage
- MinIO (local dev)
- Any S3-compatible provider

## File Validation

### Frontend Validation
- MIME type: application/pdf, application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document
- File size: <= 10MB
- Immediate feedback before upload attempt

### Backend Validation
- Verify contentType parameter matches allowed types
- Do NOT trust file extension alone
- Optional: Magic number validation (first bytes of file)

## Replace Resume Flow
1. Candidate already has resumeKey
2. User clicks "Replace Resume"
3. Same upload flow as new resume
4. Old resumeKey is overwritten on candidate record
5. Old file can remain in storage (or cleanup job later)
6. Audit log: RESUME_REPLACED

## Dependencies
- Requires: Phase A (auth), Phase B (permissions), Phase D (candidate record)
- Blocks: None (but needed for complete candidate workflow)

## Environment Setup
Required environment variables:
- S3_BUCKET or SUPABASE_STORAGE_BUCKET
- S3_REGION (if AWS)
- S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID, S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY (or IAM role)
- SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY (if Supabase)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Resume uploads successfully to private storage
- Resume downloads via signed URL only
- Unsigned storage URLs return 403
- File type validation rejects invalid types
- File size validation rejects oversized files
- Replace resume works correctly
- VIEWER can view/download but not upload
- All operations create audit logs
- No storage keys leak to client beyond API response
close_reason: Resume Flow epic complete: API endpoints, upload component, view/download UI on candidate detail, and form integration all implemented.

title: F.1: Implement POST /api/upload/resume endpoint
description:
# Task
Create API endpoint that generates signed upload URL for resume.

## Endpoint
POST /api/upload/resume

## Request Body
\`\`\`typescript
{
  filename: string       // original filename
  contentType: string    // mime type
  candidateId?: string   // optional, for context
}
\`\`\`

## Response
\`\`\`typescript
{
  uploadUrl: string      // signed PUT URL
  objectKey: string      // key to store with candidate
  expiresAt: string      // when URL expires
}
\`\`\`

## Business Logic
1. Verify authenticated session
2. Check ADMIN or RECRUITER role
3. Validate contentType:
   - application/pdf
   - application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document
4. Generate unique objectKey (UUID-based)
5. Generate signed upload URL (15 min expiry)
6. Return URL and key

## Security Requirements
- Storage bucket must be private
- Signed URL only valid for specified content-type
- URL expires in 15 minutes
- Object key is unguessable (UUID)

## File Location
Uses lib/storage.ts utilities (B.5)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Returns valid signed upload URL
- URL allows PUT with correct content-type
- Invalid content-type returns 400
- VIEWER gets 403
- Unauthenticated gets 401

```typescript
// Example logging utility
export class TestLogger {
  async step(name: string, fn: () => Promise<void>) {
    console.log(`[STEP] ${name} - START ${new Date().toISOString()}`)
    const start = Date.now()
    try {
      await fn()
      console.log(`[STEP] ${name} - PASS (${Date.now() - start}ms)`)
    } catch (error) {
      console.log(`[STEP] ${name} - FAIL (${Date.now() - start}ms)`)
      throw error
    }
  }
}
```

- Enable trace recording on failure

- Retry configuration for flaky tests

title: Phase A: Foundation
description:
# Overview
Initialize the core technical foundation for the HR Recruiting Dashboard. This phase establishes 
the project skeleton, database layer, authentication system, and basic navigation structure that 
all subsequent phases will build upon.

## Strategic Importance
This is the highest-priority phase because:
1. **Blocking dependency**: Every other phase requires the auth system, database, and app shell
2. **Risk reduction**: Early validation of tech stack choices (Next.js 14+, Prisma, Auth.js)
3. **Developer experience**: Sets up TypeScript, linting, and conventions that compound over time

## Key Deliverables
- Next.js App Router project with TypeScript strict mode
- Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui component library configured
- PostgreSQL database with Prisma ORM
- Complete schema with all 5 entities (User, Job, Candidate, Application, AuditLog)
- Auth.js with session-based authentication and role handling
- Protected route middleware
- App shell with sidebar navigation

## Success Criteria
- `npm run dev` starts without errors
- Database migrations apply cleanly
- Seed data loads successfully (3 users, 12 jobs, 10 candidates)
- Login flow works with test credentials
- Protected routes redirect unauthenticated users
- Role is accessible in server and client components

## Technical Decisions
- Using App Router (not Pages Router) for:
  - Server Components by default (better security, smaller bundles)
  - Nested layouts for consistent shell
  - Route Groups for organization
- Using Prisma over raw SQL for:
  - Type safety end-to-end
  - Migration management
  - Relation handling
- Session-based auth over JWT for:
  - Easier revocation
  - Server-side role checks without token parsing
  - HttpOnly cookies for security

## Estimated Effort
Medium-high: 2-3 days for experienced developer
close_reason: Phase A Foundation complete: Next.js 16 + TypeScript, Tailwind/shadcn, Prisma + PostgreSQL schema (5 entities), Auth.js, middleware, app shell, seed data

title: A.1: Initialize Next.js project with TypeScript
description:
# Task
Bootstrap a new Next.js 14+ application using the App Router with TypeScript in strict mode.

## Why This Matters
- App Router is required for server components (security, performance)
- TypeScript strict mode catches type errors early, preventing runtime bugs
- This establishes the project foundation that everything else builds on

## Implementation Steps
1. Run `npx create-next-app@latest hr-dashboard --typescript --tailwind --eslint --app --src-dir`
2. Update `tsconfig.json` to enable strict mode:
   \`\`\`json
   {
     "compilerOptions": {
       "strict": true,
       "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true
     }
   }
   \`\`\`
3. Verify dev server starts: `npm run dev`
4. Create initial folder structure under `src/`:
   - `src/app/` (App Router pages)
   - `src/components/` (shared components)

   - `src/types/` (TypeScript types)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `npm run dev` starts without errors
- `npm run build` completes without type errors
- Project uses App Router (no pages/ directory)
- TypeScript strict mode is enabled
- Folder structure matches project structure spec

## Technical Notes
- Choose Next.js 14.x or later for stable Server Actions
- Using `--src-dir` keeps root clean for configs
- Tailwind flag sets up initial styling foundation

title: A.3: Set up PostgreSQL with Prisma ORM
description:
# Task
Configure Prisma ORM with PostgreSQL for type-safe database access and migration management.

## Why This Matters
- Prisma generates TypeScript types from schema, ensuring type safety end-to-end
- Migrations provide version-controlled schema changes
- Relation handling simplifies queries across Job/Candidate/Application

## Implementation Steps
1. Install Prisma:
   \`\`\`bash
   npm install prisma @prisma/client
   npx prisma init
   \`\`\`

2. Configure `.env`:
   \`\`\`
   DATABASE_URL="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/hr_dashboard?schema=public"
   \`\`\`

3. Create `prisma/schema.prisma` with all 5 entities (see next task for full schema)

4. Set up Prisma client singleton in `src/lib/prisma.ts`:
   \`\`\`typescript
   import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client'
   
   const globalForPrisma = globalThis as unknown as { prisma: PrismaClient }
   
   export const prisma = globalForPrisma.prisma || new PrismaClient()
   
   if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') globalForPrisma.prisma = prisma
   \`\`\`

5. Add Prisma scripts to `package.json`:
   \`\`\`json
   {
     "db:generate": "prisma generate",
     "db:push": "prisma db push",
     "db:migrate": "prisma migrate dev",
     "db:seed": "prisma db seed",
     "db:studio": "prisma studio"
   }
   \`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- Prisma client generates without errors
- Can connect to PostgreSQL database
- `npx prisma studio` opens database viewer
- Prisma client is properly singleton-ized

## Local Development Setup
For local PostgreSQL:
- Docker: `docker run -d -p 5432:5432 -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres postgres:15`
- Or use local PostgreSQL installation
- Or use Railway/Supabase for hosted dev database

## Notes
- The singleton pattern prevents too many connections in dev (hot reload)
- Schema definition is separate task to keep this focused on infrastructure
close_reason: Prisma setup complete: packages installed, scripts added, prisma.ts singleton created

title: A.4: Define complete database schema
description:
# Task
Create the full Prisma schema with all 5 entities: User, Job, Candidate, Application, AuditLog.

## Why This Matters
- The data model is the foundation of the entire application
- Getting relations right now prevents painful migrations later
- Indexes ensure acceptable query performance as data grows

## Schema Definition

### User Entity
\`\`\`prisma
model User {
  id           String     @id @default(uuid())
  name         String
  email        String     @unique
  passwordHash String
  role         UserRole   @default(VIEWER)
  active       Boolean    @default(true)
  createdAt    DateTime   @default(now())
  updatedAt    DateTime   @updatedAt
  
  auditLogs    AuditLog[]
}

enum UserRole {
  ADMIN
  RECRUITER
  VIEWER
}
\`\`\`

### Job Entity
\`\`\`prisma
model Job {
  id              String          @id @default(uuid())
  title           String
  department      String
  description     String          @db.Text
  location        String?
  hiringManager   String?
  recruiterOwner  String?
  status          JobStatus       @default(OPEN)
  priority        JobPriority     @default(MEDIUM)
  pipelineHealth  PipelineHealth?
  isCritical      Boolean         @default(false)
  openedAt        DateTime?
  targetFillDate  DateTime?
  closedAt        DateTime?
  createdAt       DateTime        @default(now())
  updatedAt       DateTime        @updatedAt
  
  applications    Application[]
  
  @@index([status])
  @@index([isCritical])
  @@index([pipelineHealth])
  @@index([updatedAt])
  @@index([targetFillDate])
}

enum JobStatus {
  OPEN
  CLOSED
  ON_HOLD
}

enum JobPriority {
  LOW
  MEDIUM
  HIGH
  CRITICAL
}

enum PipelineHealth {
  AHEAD
  ON_TRACK
  BEHIND
}
\`\`\`

### Candidate Entity
\`\`\`prisma
model Candidate {
  id             String        @id @default(uuid())
  firstName      String
  lastName       String
  email          String?
  phone          String?
  linkedinUrl    String?
  currentCompany String?
  location       String?
  source         CandidateSource?
  resumeKey      String?
  resumeName     String?
  notes          String?       @db.Text
  createdAt      DateTime      @default(now())
  updatedAt      DateTime      @updatedAt
  
  applications   Application[]
  
  @@index([lastName])
  @@index([email])
}

enum CandidateSource {
  REFERRAL
  LINKEDIN
  CAREERS_PAGE
  AGENCY
  OTHER
}
\`\`\`

### Application Entity
\`\`\`prisma
model Application {
  id              String             @id @default(uuid())
  jobId           String
  candidateId     String
  stage           ApplicationStage   @default(NEW)
  recruiterOwner  String?
  interviewNotes  String?            @db.Text
  stageUpdatedAt  DateTime           @default(now())
  createdAt       DateTime           @default(now())
  updatedAt       DateTime           @updatedAt
  
  job             Job                @relation(fields: [jobId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  candidate       Candidate          @relation(fields: [candidateId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  
  @@unique([jobId, candidateId])
  @@index([jobId])
  @@index([candidateId])
  @@index([stage])
}

enum ApplicationStage {
  NEW
  SCREENING
  INTERVIEWING
  FINAL_ROUND
  OFFER
  HIRED
  REJECTED
  WITHDRAWN
}
\`\`\`

### AuditLog Entity
\`\`\`prisma
model AuditLog {
  id          String   @id @default(uuid())
  userId      String?
  action      String
  entityType  String
  entityId    String
  beforeJson  Json?
  afterJson   Json?
  ipAddress   String?
  createdAt   DateTime @default(now())
  
  user        User?    @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: SetNull)
  
  @@index([entityType, entityId])
  @@index([createdAt])
}
\`\`\`

## Implementation Steps
1. Create `prisma/schema.prisma` with complete schema above
2. Run `npx prisma generate` to generate client
3. Run `npx prisma migrate dev --name init` to create initial migration
4. Verify tables created in database

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 5 entities defined with correct fields
- All enums defined
- All relations established
- All indexes created
- Migration applies cleanly
- Prisma client types generated

## Constraints Verified
- User.email unique
- Application(jobId, candidateId) unique
- onDelete behaviors correct (Cascade for Application, SetNull for AuditLog.user)
close_reason: Complete Prisma schema with all 5 entities (User, Job, Candidate, Application, AuditLog), all enums, relations, and indexes. Prisma client generated successfully.

title: A.5: Create seed data script
description:
# Task
Create a Prisma seed script that populates the database with realistic test data for development.

## Why This Matters
- Developers need data to test all screens immediately
- Seed data exercises edge cases (critical jobs, multiple stages, etc.)
- Consistent seed data helps reproduce issues

## Seed Data Requirements (From Handoff)

### Users (3 minimum)
| Name | Email | Role | Password (dev only) |
|------|-------|------|---------------------|
| Admin User | admin@company.com | ADMIN | admin123 |
| Jane Recruiter | jane.recruiter@company.com | RECRUITER | recruiter123 |
| Bob Viewer | bob.viewer@company.com | VIEWER | viewer123 |

### Jobs (12 minimum)
Distribution:
- 4 OPEN + CRITICAL (appear in top 10)
- 4 OPEN + not critical
- 2 CLOSED
- 2 ON_HOLD

Pipeline Health Distribution (for OPEN jobs):
- 3 AHEAD
- 4 ON_TRACK  
- 1 BEHIND

Include variety in:
- Departments (Engineering, Marketing, Sales, Operations, HR)
- Locations (Remote, NYC, SF, Boston)
- Target fill dates (some past, some future, some null)

### Candidates (10 minimum)
Include variety in:
- Sources (all enum values represented)
- With/without email
- With/without LinkedIn
- With/without resume (resumeKey)

### Applications
- At least 20 total applications
- Every stage represented at least once
- At least one candidate linked to 3+ jobs
- Enough to populate critical jobs with candidate counts

## Implementation Notes
- Use `prisma/seed.ts` with ts-node
- Hash passwords with bcrypt (same as auth will use)
- Use realistic but fake data (not real people)
- Make seed idempotent (can re-run without duplicates)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `npm run db:seed` populates all tables
- Dashboard shows meaningful data
- Critical jobs list shows 4+ jobs
- All three pipeline health states visible
- Can log in with each test user
close_reason: Added idempotent Prisma seed script, configured Prisma 7 seed command, and verified db push + seed against local Postgres with 3 users, 12 jobs, 10 candidates, and 21 applications

title: A.6: Configure Auth.js with credentials provider
description:
# Task
Set up Auth.js (NextAuth v5) with session-based authentication and role handling.

## Why This Matters
- Auth is the security foundation - must be correct from the start
- Session-based auth enables server-side role checks without token parsing
- HttpOnly cookies prevent XSS token theft

## Key Implementation Points
- Credentials provider for email/password login
- Session strategy: database or JWT (database preferred for revocation)
- Session callback includes userId and role
- Secure cookie settings for production
- Login/logout API routes

## Session Shape
\`\`\`typescript
interface Session {
  user: {
    id: string
    email: string
    name: string
    role: 'ADMIN' | 'RECRUITER' | 'VIEWER'
  }
}
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- Login with valid credentials succeeds
- Login with invalid credentials fails with error message
- Session is accessible in server components
- Session is accessible in API routes
- Role is included in session
- Logout clears session
close_reason: Auth.js v5 configured with credentials provider, JWT session strategy, role handling, login page created

title: A.7: Implement route protection middleware

# Task
Create Next.js middleware that protects all routes except /login, redirecting unauthenticated users.

## Why This Matters

- Middleware runs before page rendering (efficient)
- Consistent redirect behavior across app

## Protected Routes
- / (dashboard)
- /jobs/*
- /candidates/*
- /api/* (except auth endpoints)

## Public Routes
- /login
- /api/auth/*

## Middleware Logic
1. Check if route matches public patterns → allow
2. Check for valid session → allow if exists
3. Redirect to /login with return URL

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unauthenticated user visiting / redirects to /login
- After login, user redirects back to intended page
- API routes return 401 instead of redirect
- Public routes accessible without auth

title: Phase H: Security and Polish
description:
# Overview
Harden the application security, verify all protection mechanisms work correctly, and 
polish the user experience with proper state handling and accessibility.

## Security Hardening

### Rate Limiting
Apply rate limits to prevent abuse:

| Endpoint Type | Limit | Window |
|--------------|-------|--------|
| Auth (login) | 5 attempts | 15 min |
| Write endpoints | 30 requests | 1 min |
| Upload endpoints | 10 uploads | 1 min |
| General API | 100 requests | 1 min |

Implementation options:
- Vercel Edge middleware (if hosting on Vercel)
- upstash/ratelimit (serverless-friendly)
- Express rate-limit (if custom server)

### Security Headers
Required headers (via Next.js config or middleware):

\`\`\`javascript
{
  'X-Frame-Options': 'DENY',
  'X-Content-Type-Options': 'nosniff',
  'Strict-Transport-Security': 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains',
  'Content-Security-Policy': "default-src 'self'; ...",
  'Referrer-Policy': 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin',
  'Permissions-Policy': 'camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()'
}
\`\`\`

### Auth Enforcement Audit
Verify every protected route and endpoint:
- [ ] All /api/* endpoints check session
- [ ] All page.tsx files verify authentication
- [ ] Write endpoints verify ADMIN/RECRUITER role
- [ ] Middleware redirects unauthenticated users
- [ ] No data leaks in error messages

### Audit Log Verification
Confirm audit logging for ALL write operations:
- JOB_CREATED, JOB_UPDATED, JOB_CLOSED
- CANDIDATE_CREATED, CANDIDATE_UPDATED
- APPLICATION_CREATED, APPLICATION_UPDATED, APPLICATION_DELETED
- RESUME_UPLOADED, RESUME_REPLACED

## UX Polish

### Empty States
Every list/table needs graceful empty state:
- No jobs: "No jobs found. Create your first job to get started."
- No candidates: "No candidates yet. Add your first candidate."
- No applications on job: "No candidates attached to this job yet."
- No resume: "No resume uploaded. Upload a resume to continue."
- Search no results: "No results for 'X'. Try different keywords."

### Loading States
Implement skeleton loading for:
- Dashboard cards and tables
- Job/candidate lists
- Detail page sections

### Error States
Handle and display:
- Network failures: "Unable to load data. Please try again."
- Not found: "Job not found. It may have been deleted."
- Forbidden: "You don't have permission to perform this action."
- Validation errors: Inline field-level messages

### Accessibility
- All form inputs have labels
- Focus states are visible
- Color is never the only indicator
- Tab order is logical
- Buttons/links are keyboard accessible
- ARIA labels where needed

## Keyboard Navigation
- Tab through form fields
- Enter to submit forms
- Escape to close modals
- Table row navigation (optional enhancement)

## Dependencies
- Requires: All previous phases (C, D, E, F, G) substantially complete
- This phase is about verification and hardening, not new features

## Acceptance Criteria
- Rate limiting blocks excessive requests
- Security headers present on all responses
- Every protected route verified to require auth
- Every write endpoint verified to check role
- Audit logs exist for all write operations
- Empty states render correctly
- Loading states show skeletons
- Error states show user-friendly messages
- Tab navigation works through all forms
- Focus states are visible
- No accessibility warnings in Lighthouse
close_reason: Phase H Security and Polish complete: Auth audit with RBAC fixes, security headers configured, audit logging verified, empty states implemented, loading skeletons present, error boundaries added, accessibility verified. Rate limiting deferred pending Redis infrastructure.

title: H.1: Implement rate limiting
description:
# Task
Add rate limiting to protect against abuse.

## Rate Limits by Endpoint Type

| Endpoint Type | Limit | Window | Behavior |
|--------------|-------|--------|----------|
| Auth (login) | 5 attempts | 15 min | Block |
| Write endpoints | 30 requests | 1 min | Block |
| Upload endpoints | 10 uploads | 1 min | Block |
| General API | 100 requests | 1 min | Block |

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Vercel Edge (if on Vercel)
Use Vercel's built-in rate limiting.

### Option B: Upstash Rate Limit
\`\`\`typescript
import { Ratelimit } from '@upstash/ratelimit'
import { Redis } from '@upstash/redis'

const ratelimit = new Ratelimit({
  redis: Redis.fromEnv(),
  limiter: Ratelimit.slidingWindow(30, '1 m'),
})
\`\`\`

### Option C: In-Memory (dev only)
Simple Map-based limiter for development.

## Response on Rate Limit
\`\`\`json
{
  "error": "Too many requests. Please try again later.",
  "retryAfter": 60
}
\`\`\`
Status: 429 Too Many Requests
Header: Retry-After: 60

## Acceptance Criteria
- Excessive requests are blocked
- 429 response with retry info
- Different limits for different endpoint types
- Login has stricter limits
- Normal usage is not affected
close_reason: Deferred: Rate limiting requires Redis/Upstash infrastructure not currently in stack. Recommend using @upstash/ratelimit when ready. Current security relies on auth, RBAC, and security headers which provide baseline protection.

title: H.2: Configure security headers
description:
# Task
Add security headers to all responses.

## Required Headers

\`\`\`typescript
{
  'X-Frame-Options': 'DENY',
  'X-Content-Type-Options': 'nosniff',
  'Strict-Transport-Security': 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains',
  'Referrer-Policy': 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin',
  'Permissions-Policy': 'camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()',
  'Content-Security-Policy': "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: blob: https:; connect-src 'self' https:;"
}
\`\`\`

## Implementation in Next.js

### Option A: next.config.js
\`\`\`javascript
const securityHeaders = [...]
module.exports = {
  async headers() {
    return [{ source: '/:path*', headers: securityHeaders }]
  }
}
\`\`\`

### Option B: Middleware
Apply headers in middleware.ts.

## CSP Considerations
- 'unsafe-inline' for styled-components/emotion
- 'unsafe-eval' may be needed for dev
- Tighten for production

## Verification
- Use securityheaders.com to scan
- Chrome DevTools → Network → check headers

## Acceptance Criteria
- All headers present on responses
- No XSS via inline scripts blocked
- No clickjacking possible
- HTTPS enforced in production
- Security scan passes
close_reason: Security headers fully configured in next.config.ts: CSP with restrictive directives, X-Frame-Options DENY, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, and HSTS in production.

title: H.7: Implement error states and handling
description:
# Task
Add proper error handling and error state displays.

## Error Types

### Network/Fetch Errors
- API unreachable
- Timeout
- Message: "Unable to load data. Please try again."

### Not Found (404)
- Invalid job ID
- Invalid candidate ID
- Message: "Not found. It may have been deleted."

### Forbidden (403)
- Unauthorized action attempt
- Message: "You don't have permission to do this."

### Validation Errors (400)
- Invalid form data
- Show inline field errors

### Server Errors (500)
- Unexpected error
- Message: "Something went wrong. Please try again."
- Log to Sentry

## Implementation

### Error Boundary
\`\`\`tsx
<ErrorBoundary fallback={<ErrorState />}>
  <PageContent />
</ErrorBoundary>
\`\`\`

### API Error Handling
\`\`\`typescript
try {
  const data = await fetchJobs()
} catch (error) {
  if (error.status === 404) return <NotFound />
  if (error.status === 403) return <Unauthorized />
  return <ErrorState retry={refetch} />
}
\`\`\`

### Never Expose
- Stack traces
- Internal error messages
- Database errors
- File paths

## Error State Component (B.7)
- Error icon
- User-friendly message
- Retry button (if retryable)
- Back/home link

## Acceptance Criteria
- All error scenarios handled
- User-friendly messages only
- No sensitive data exposed
- Retry works when available
- 404 pages render correctly
- Error boundary catches crashes
close_reason: Implemented error handling: error.tsx for route error boundary with reset, not-found.tsx for 404 pages, global-error.tsx for critical errors. All with appropriate UI, error logging, and retry functionality.

title: Phase G: Dashboard
description:
# Overview
Build the main dashboard page that provides an at-a-glance view of hiring health.
The dashboard is the primary entry point for HR users and must communicate status immediately.

## UX Principles (From Handoff)
- **Two-click depth**: Dashboard → Job Detail → Candidate Detail
- **Status at a glance**: Visually scannable within seconds
- **Clean over clever**: Simple, robust, readable UI

## Dashboard Sections

### Section A: KPI Cards
Four primary metrics displayed as prominent cards:

| Metric | Definition | Visual |
|--------|------------|--------|
| Jobs Open | count(Job WHERE status='OPEN') | Large number, maybe trend |
| Jobs Closed | count(Job WHERE status='CLOSED') | Large number |
| Active Critical Jobs | count(Job WHERE status='OPEN' AND isCritical=true) | Large number, alert color if > 0 |
| Active Candidates | count(DISTINCT candidate_id FROM Application JOIN Job WHERE Job.status='OPEN') | Large number |

### Section B: Pipeline Summary
Visual representation of pipeline health distribution:

| Health | Color | Count |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Ahead | Green | N |
| On Track | Amber | N |
| Behind | Red | N |

Display options:
- Three horizontal pills/cards
- Segmented bar chart with proportions
- Simple stat row

Only count OPEN jobs in pipeline health summary.

### Section C: Top 10 Critical Jobs
Spotlight table for jobs needing immediate attention:

**Query:**
\`\`\`sql
SELECT j.*, COUNT(a.id) as activeCount
FROM Job j
LEFT JOIN Application a ON a.jobId = j.id 
  AND a.stage NOT IN ('REJECTED', 'WITHDRAWN')
WHERE j.status = 'OPEN' AND j.isCritical = true
GROUP BY j.id
ORDER BY 
  j.targetFillDate ASC NULLS LAST,
  j.updatedAt DESC
LIMIT 10
\`\`\`

**Columns:**
- Job Title (clickable)
- Department
- Recruiter Owner
- Target Fill Date
- Pipeline Health Badge
- Active Candidate Count

### Section D: All Jobs Table
Full jobs list integrated into dashboard:

**Columns:**
- Title (clickable)
- Department
- Status Badge
- Critical Flag
- Pipeline Health Badge
- Active Candidates
- Date Opened
- Target Fill Date

**Interactions:**
- Search by title
- Filter by status (multi-select)
- Filter by department (multi-select)
- Sort by multiple columns
- "New Job" button for ADMIN/RECRUITER

**Default Sort:**
- OPEN jobs first
- Then by updatedAt DESC

## API Endpoint

### GET /api/dashboard/stats
Returns aggregated dashboard data in single response:

\`\`\`typescript
interface DashboardStats {
  jobsOpen: number;
  jobsClosed: number;
  activeCriticalJobs: number;
  activeCandidates: number;
  pipelineHealth: {
    ahead: number;
    onTrack: number;
    behind: number;
  };
  criticalJobs: CriticalJobSummary[];
}
\`\`\`

This reduces client-side requests and ensures consistent calculations.

## Performance Considerations
- Dashboard is the most frequently loaded page
- Consider caching stats with short TTL (30-60 seconds)
- Aggregate queries should use indexes on:
  - Job.status
  - Job.isCritical
  - Job.pipelineHealth
  - Application.jobId

## Dependencies
- Requires: Phase C (Jobs API), Phase E (Applications for candidate counts)
- Also uses: Phase B (status badges, KPI card components)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Dashboard loads within 2 seconds
- KPI cards show accurate counts
- Pipeline summary shows correct distribution
- Critical jobs list shows max 10, correctly sorted
- All jobs table supports search/filter/sort
- Clicking job title navigates to job detail
- "New Job" button visible only for ADMIN/RECRUITER
- Empty states display correctly when no data
- Loading skeleton shows during data fetch
close_reason: Phase G Dashboard epic complete: Stats API, pipeline summary, dashboard page with all widgets fully implemented.

title: G.1: Implement GET /api/dashboard/stats endpoint
description:
# Task
Create API endpoint returning all dashboard statistics in a single response.

## Endpoint
GET /api/dashboard/stats

## Response
\`\`\`typescript
interface DashboardStats {
  jobsOpen: number
  jobsClosed: number
  activeCriticalJobs: number
  activeCandidates: number
  pipelineHealth: {
    ahead: number
    onTrack: number
    behind: number
  }
  criticalJobs: CriticalJobSummary[]
}

interface CriticalJobSummary {
  id: string
  title: string
  department: string
  recruiterOwner: string | null
  targetFillDate: string | null
  pipelineHealth: PipelineHealth
  activeCandidateCount: number
}
\`\`\`

## Queries

### KPI Counts
\`\`\`typescript
// Jobs Open
count(Job WHERE status = 'OPEN')

// Jobs Closed  
count(Job WHERE status = 'CLOSED')

// Active Critical Jobs
count(Job WHERE status = 'OPEN' AND isCritical = true)

// Active Candidates (distinct candidates linked to open jobs)
count(DISTINCT candidateId FROM Application 
  JOIN Job ON Job.id = Application.jobId 
  WHERE Job.status = 'OPEN')
\`\`\`

### Pipeline Health Counts
\`\`\`typescript
// Group by pipelineHealth where status = OPEN
groupBy(Job WHERE status = 'OPEN', pipelineHealth)
\`\`\`

### Critical Jobs (Top 10)
\`\`\`typescript
SELECT j.*, COUNT(a.id) as activeCandidateCount
FROM Job j
LEFT JOIN Application a ON a.jobId = j.id 
  AND a.stage NOT IN ('REJECTED', 'WITHDRAWN')
WHERE j.status = 'OPEN' AND j.isCritical = true
GROUP BY j.id
ORDER BY j.targetFillDate ASC NULLS LAST, j.updatedAt DESC
LIMIT 10
\`\`\`

## Performance
- Use efficient aggregate queries
- Consider caching with short TTL (30-60 seconds)
- Ensure proper indexes exist

## Acceptance Criteria
- Returns all required stats
- Counts are accurate
- Critical jobs sorted correctly
- Nulls last for targetFillDate
- Response time < 500ms
- Unauthenticated returns 401
close_reason: Implemented GET /api/dashboard/stats with auth guard, KPI counts, pipeline aggregates, critical jobs summary; lint, tsc, and next build verified.
